topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat accusingly on the screen, a stark white rectangle against the muted backdrop of my dorm room. I reread the rejection letter for the prestigious scholarship three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, twitching slightly before finally resting on the mouse. The silence in the room was heavy, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I stood up abruptly, pacing the worn rug, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, taking on a sharper, more defined edge. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting someone to materialize, or perhaps just the sound of my own footsteps to become deafening.

[story 2]

I traced the edges of the acceptance letter with a fingertip, the glossy paper cool beneath my touch. A strange hollowness bloomed in my stomach, a sensation like a sudden drop in temperature. I tried to focus on the textbooks piled on my desk, but the equations swam before my eyes, replaced by the relentless looping of the denial. I started stacking books haphazardly, the movements jerky and uncoordinated. When I finally looked up, my gaze drifted to the window. The world outside seemed too bright, too loud, the sounds of campus life mocking the quiet dread settling deep in my bones. I didn't move to tidy up; I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling strangely disconnected from the air around me.

[story 3]

He sat at the kitchen table, staring into his lukewarm coffee. The denial had landed like a physical blow, knocking the air from his lungs. He picked up his phone, intending to call a friend, but his thumb froze above the screen. Instead, he just held the device, the glass reflecting a distorted image of his own face—pale, drawn. He pushed the chair back violently, the scrape of wood against the tile echoing loudly in the small apartment. He walked to the window and pressed his forehead against the cool glass, letting out a slow, ragged exhale that seemed to carry all the weight of his disappointment. For a long moment, he didn't blink, just absorbing the stark reality of the situation.
